content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 25, 2000

Mr. Earl Rush
505 Diamond Bar Lane
Roseville, California 95678

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-00-216

Dear Mr. Rush:

This letter is in response to your request for assistance regarding the provisions of
the Political Reform Act ("the Act").¹

QUESTION

As a city council member, may you sign campaign literature opposing a ballot
measure amending the city municipal code, where you sign the campaign literature as a
council member, but the literature is not on city stationery or in any other way at city
expense?

CONCLUSION

Nothing in the Act prohibits you from signing campaign literature opposing a
ballot measure and using your title of council member, where the campaign literature will
be paid for by a political committee opposing the measure, and is not printed on city
stationery or at city expense.

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91015. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18996, of the California Code of Regulations. Because you have requested general guidance about
the Act's campaign provisions, we are providing you with informal assistance indicated by the file number
prefix "I." Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity conferred by an opinion
or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-00-216
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are a council member for the City of Roseville. You want to know whether
you may sign campaign literature opposing a ballot measure that amends the city
municipal code. You wish to sign the campaign literature using your title as council
member. The mailer will not be printed on city stationery or published in any other way
at city expense. The mailer will be paid for by a political committee that opposes the
ballot measure.

ANALYSIS

The Act defines a "contribution" as a payment, unless it is clear from the
surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes. (Section 82015.) A
payment is made for political purposes if it is for the purpose of influencing or attempting
to influence the action of the voters for or against the qualification or passage of any
measure. (Regulation 18215(a)(1).)

An "expenditure" is any monetary or nonmonetary payment made for political
purposes. (Section 82025.) "Expenditure" includes any monetary or nonmonetary
payment that is used for communications which expressly advocate² the qualification,
passage or defeat of a clearly identified ballot measure. A measure that has qualified to
be placed on the ballot is clearly identified if the communication states a proposition
number, official title or popular name associated with the measure. In addition, the
measure is clearly identified if the communication refers to the subject matter of the
measure and either states that the measure is before the people for a vote or, taken as a
whole and in context, unambiguously refers to the measure. (Regulation 18225(b).)

Elected officials do not give up their First Amendment rights to speak out on
public issues upon being elected to office. (City of Fairfield v. Superior Court of Solano
County (1975) 14 Cal.3d. 768, 780-782.) There is nothing in the Act that prohibits an
elected official from signing a piece of campaign literature using his or her title as an
elected official. You state that the mailer opposing the ballot measure will be sent out by
a political committee that opposes the measure. It will not be sent out on city stationery
or using any city funds. In that case, the political committee sending the mailer has the
obligation to report the expenditure for the mailer on its regularly filed campaign
disclosure reports.

In addition, regulation 18215(c)(7) provides that a payment made by a ballot
measure committee for a communication in which the ballot measure supported or
opposed by the committee is endorsed or opposed by a candidate, and the communication
does not expressly advocate the nomination or election of the endorsing candidate or the

² A communication "expressly advocates" the qualification, passage or defeat of a measure if it
contains express words of advocacy such as "vote for," "elect," "support," "cast your ballot," "vote against,"
"defeat," "reject," "sign petitions for" or otherwise refers to a clearly identified candidate or measure so that
the communication, taken as a whole, unambiguously urges a particular result in an election. (Regulation
18225(b)(2).)

File No. I-00-216
Page No. 3

defeat of an opponent of the endorsing candidate does not constitute a contribution to the
candidate. (See Wax v. Fair Political Practices Commission, United States District
Court, Eastern District of California, Case No. CIV. S-90-1232-LKK.)

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
